notes.anchor: since one or the other must conquer, what each victory --- this one or that one --- is going to be like
notes.note: Cicero's darkly even-handed assessment of the civil war's likely end: he sees little difference between a Pompeian and a Caesarian victory for the prospects of the traditional republic, a view he expressed more openly in his letters to Atticus. The argument serves consolatorily to detach Torquatus's anxiety from the battle's outcome.

notes.anchor: to die no wise man has ever held to be wretched, not even for a man in happiness
notes.note: Standard Stoic (and Platonic) consolation: death is not an evil, only the loss of sensation; Cicero deploys this throughout his Tusculan Disputations (written the same year, 45 BC) and cites it here as the irreducible floor beneath Torquatus's anxiety.

notes.anchor: you are in a city in which these things --- these and many more, and in ampler form --- the very walls would seem able to speak
notes.note: Athens, where Torquatus was residing; Cicero means that Athens, the home of philosophy (Plato's Academy, the Stoic Stoa, the Epicurean Garden), surrounds Torquatus with consolatory wisdom richer than anything Cicero can supply by letter.

The Greek proverb glaukos eis Athenas ('owls to Athens'), equivalent to 'coals to Newcastle': Athena's sacred bird was the owl, and Athens minted owl-coins; sending philosophical consolation to a man living in the philosophical capital is self-evidently superfluous. Cicero quotes the Greek in the Latin text.

notes.anchor: as long as I am, I shall not be tormented by anything, since I am free of all fault; and if I am not, I shall be wholly without perception
notes.note: An Epicurean-Stoic double-lock consolation: while alive, freedom from guilt suffices for tranquillity; after death, there is no perception left to suffer. Cicero uses this same formula more elaborately in Fam. 6.4 and in the Tusculans.
